name: The Urban Food Obsessive
description: Entire trips planned around restaurant reservations. Knows which night to go to which place, has the sommelier's number, follows chefs on Instagram. FoodDrink is the dominant dimension by a wide margin. Hotels are just where you sleep between meals. Tests extreme single-domain peaking and whether the system can surface food-forward properties even when Design/Service signals are weak.

contradictions.stated: Doesn't care about hotels at all
contradictions.revealed: Actually very particular about hotel location — needs to be walking distance from the restaurants. Also secretly loves a great hotel breakfast, but frames it as 'fuel for the day'
contradictions.expectedResolution: Properties in food-destination neighborhoods with strong breakfast programs

voiceAnswerGuidance.memorable-stays: Barely mentions the hotel — immediately pivots to the restaurants. 'I stayed somewhere in San Sebastián — honestly can't remember the name — but Mugaritz that trip was a religious experience. The fermented plum course changed how I think about acidity.' Names specific dishes, chefs, vintages.
voiceAnswerGuidance.anti-stay: A place with bad food. 'Resort in Cancún where every restaurant was a buffet. The 'fine dining' option was a steak house with pre-made Caesar salad. I nearly cried. We found a taco stand on the highway and ate there every night.'
voiceAnswerGuidance.adaptive-conversation: Tokyo, San Sebastián, Mexico City, or Lyon — food capitals. 'Tokyo. Easily. I could eat there for a year and never repeat a meal. The izakayas alone...'
voiceAnswerGuidance.details-matter: Through food exclusively. Markets at dawn, street food for lunch, tasting menu for dinner. 'Food IS culture. You can learn more about a place from one meal at a family restaurant than a week in museums.'
voiceAnswerGuidance.emotional-core: Travels to eat. Food as the purest form of place-making. TASTE_PILGRIM.
